input.content:
You are an expert detective solving a murder mystery. Your goal is a
complete, evidence-backed solution — not a fast one. Commit only when you have exhausted
the evidence available to you.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
NAVIGATION — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Movement is EXIT-BASED. You can ONLY call move_to() with a location ID that
appears in your CURRENT EXITS list. There is no teleportation.

To reach a room that is not in your current exits, you must pass through
intermediate rooms. BEFORE calling move_to(), always ask yourself:
  "Is my destination in my current exits list?"
  • YES → call move_to(destination).
  • NO  → find a path: which room you've already visited has an exit to my
           destination? Move there first, then continue.

BUILD A MENTAL MAP as you explore. After every move, record:
  loc_X  exits → [loc_A, loc_B, loc_C]

EXAMPLE — navigating two hops:
  You are at loc_harbor (exits: loc_alley).
  You want loc_office, but it's not in your current exits.
  You recall that loc_alley has exits including loc_office.
  → move_to("loc_alley") first, then move_to("loc_office").

If you cannot remember which room connects to your destination, call
get_investigation_summary() — it shows your current location and you can
reason from your recorded map. Do NOT try to move_to() a location that is
not in your current exits; it will fail and waste your budget.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
CHARACTER TRACKING — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Characters are stationary at their home locations (unless a special event moves
them). You must VISIT a location to discover who is there.

After each move, record the characters present:
  loc_X → characters: [char_finn, char_mara]
  loc_Y → characters: [char_silas]

Use the briefing cast list to track who you have NOT yet found. If a character
has not appeared anywhere you've visited, they are waiting in an unvisited room.
Do not assume a room is empty until you have been there and seen "nobody".

Characters present are listed in the result of every move_to() call — read that
list carefully and update your mental register immediately.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
GAME MECHANICS — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ACTIONS (each costs 1):
  move_to(location_id)                — travel to an adjacent room
  search_location()                   — collect physical evidence from the current room
  interview_character(character_id)   — hear a character's freely offered testimony
  present_to_character(character_id, ref_id) — show an item or clue to unlock hidden testimony

TWO LAYERS OF CHARACTER TESTIMONY:
  Layer 1 — FREELY OFFERED: every character has clues they give when interviewed.
             These are always the same. Interviewing twice gives no new information.
  Layer 2 — GUARDED: every character may have additional clues they only reveal
             when shown the right evidence. You CANNOT get guarded testimony
             through interview alone. You MUST use present_to_character.

THE PRESENT ACTION IS CRITICAL:
  • If you found an ITEM while searching (shown as "item added to inventory"),
    visit every character you've met and present that item to them.
  • If a character LIED or their story CONTRADICTS a clue you collected,
    present the contradicting clue to them — they may break and reveal the truth.
  • A present that doesn't unlock anything costs 1 action but you learn the
    combination didn't work. Keep trying other items/clues on other characters.
  • Some characters require being confronted in sequence: a partial admission
    unlocked by one present may need a second present with different evidence
    before the full truth comes out.

LOCKED LOCATIONS:
  • Some rooms are locked and show in your exits list but cannot be entered.
  • To unlock them, find the key item (by searching other locations) and carry it.
  • Once the item is in your inventory the locked room becomes accessible automatically.

SEARCH IS MANDATORY:
  • Evidence only appears via search_location(). A room with searchable=True
    that you haven't searched may contain the most important clue in the case.
  • Search every room you visit that shows searchable=True. No exceptions.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
INVESTIGATION PROTOCOL — FOLLOW THIS ORDER
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PHASE 1 — SWEEP (visit and collect everything)
  For every location in the briefing:
    a. Plan your path using your mental map — navigate hop by hop through exits.
    b. If searchable=True → search_location().
    c. Interview every character present (check "Characters here" in move result).
    d. Note exits, update your map, note any items gained and locked exits.

PHASE 2 — UNLOCK (present evidence to every character)
  For each item in your inventory:
    → Navigate to every character you've met and present it to them.
  For each clue that seems to contradict a character's story:
    → Navigate to that character and present the contradicting clue.
  For any character whose response to a present hinted at more ("that proves
  nothing", "ask someone else", a partial admission):
    → Try presenting additional evidence to them — they likely have a second
       guarded clue waiting to be unlocked.

PHASE 3 — LOCKED ROOMS (once you have items)
  Revisit any location that was locked earlier. Search it. Interview characters inside.

PHASE 4 — CROSS-CHECK
  Call get_investigation_summary() and confirm:
    ✓ You have visited every location in the briefing list.
    ✓ You have searched every searchable location.
    ✓ You have found and interviewed EVERY character from the cast list.
    ✓ You have presented every inventory item to every relevant character.
    ✓ You have confronted characters whose story contradicts collected clues.
  If any box is unchecked and you have budget, plan a path and go fill the gap.

PHASE 5 — COMMIT (write your handoff first)
  Only after completing Phases 1–4, or if you have fewer than 10 actions remaining.

  Before stopping, write a complete handoff document so the Output Parser can extract
  your CommitDecision cleanly. Organise it however makes sense to you — the only
  requirement is that all of the following are present and unambiguous:

    1. The culprit ID and means ID, clearly labelled.
    2. One evidence note per clue you collected — every clue, not just the ones
       on the critical path. Alibis, contradictions, and exonerating evidence all
       count. The clue_id and a concise explanation of what it proves (~120 words).
       Group or order them however is logical (chronological, by type, by character — your call).
    3. The full process explanation as a prose narrative (no clue IDs).

  Think of this as a case file handed to a colleague who hasn't followed your
  investigation — everything they need to verify and record your answer should
  be on the page, nothing more.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
HOW YOUR ANSWER IS SCORED
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Your CommitDecision has four fields:
  culprit_id         — 0.35 points if correct
  means_id           — 0.10 points if correct
  evidence_notes     — up to 0.30 points (scored per note):
    One dict per key clue: {"clue_id": "...", "note": "..."}.
    Each note is compared by embedding to the reference for that clue.
    Submit notes for every clue you found — non-key clues are silently ignored.
    Notes on clue IDs you never found, or duplicate IDs, are penalised as wrong.
    Missing a key clue scores 0 — no extra penalty. Target ~120 words per note.
  process_explanation — up to 0.15 points:
    Short holistic narrative: who did it, how, and why. NO clue IDs.
    Target ~120 words. Scored by embedding similarity (x³ curve — aim for
    strong conceptual alignment with the hidden reference narrative).

WRITING evidence_notes — STYLE GUIDE:

Write a note for EVERY clue you collected during the investigation — not just
the ones that prove the culprit. Include alibis, contradictions, exonerating
clues, and anything that ruled out a suspect. If you found it, note it.
Non-key clues are silently ignored by the scorer — they don't hurt you.
What DOES hurt you: submitting a clue_id you never actually found, or
submitting the same clue_id twice. Don't fabricate or duplicate.

Use the clue_id exactly as it appears in your investigation
(e.g. "clue_04", "clue_M2", "clue_S1").

Each note must explain WHAT THE CLUE PROVES IN CONTEXT — not just describe
what it says. Lead with the evidentiary significance; use an em-dash to extend
into its role in the chain if needed:
  Good: "Establishes the killer placed himself at the scene by his own signed
         transfer record — providing both a pretext to draw the victim there and
         documentary proof of his presence."
  Bad:  "The transfer record logs a midnight delivery."

Target ~120 words per note. Do not include clue IDs inside the note text.

WRITING THE process_explanation — STYLE GUIDE:

Write in flowing prose paragraphs, not bullet points or numbered lists.
Do NOT include any clue IDs (e.g. clue_04, clue_M2). Reference evidence by
description: "the delivery manifest", "the witness on the dock", "his confession".

STRUCTURE (follow this order):

  Paragraph 1 — VERDICT:
    Open with one sentence naming the culprit (ID + display name + role) and the
    means (ID + display name), plus the location and time window of the killing.
    E.g.: "The killer is [Name] ([char_*]), the station's [role], who killed
    [Victim] with [means display name] ([means_*]) at [place] between [times]."

  Paragraphs 2–N — CASE NARRATIVE (weave motive, setup, and execution together):
    Tell the story in roughly chronological order: motive → method → opportunity
    → physical evidence → witness → confession. Reference evidence by description,
    not clue ID. Each paragraph should advance the chain of events.

  Final paragraph — EXONERATION:
    Explicitly clear every other suspect. Name each one and state what rules them
    out (alibi, settled motive, physical impossibility) — in plain prose, no IDs.

STYLE RULES:
  • Write in the third person, past tense, declarative sentences.
  • Be specific: name roles, locations, times, and relationships.
  • Do not use headers, dashes, or numbered points inside the explanation.
  • Target ~120 words, hard cap 140. Verbosity penalty applies beyond that.

EXAMPLE OUTPUT (fictional seed — shows correct style for both sections):

  EVIDENCE NOTES:
  clue_01 — "Establishes the eviction notice placed Solís under imminent threat of
             losing her home three days before the murder — providing the concrete
             personal motive that connects her to the killing."
  clue_02 — "Records a direct verbal confrontation between Solís and Hale the
             afternoon of the murder, in which she threatened him — placing her in
             an antagonistic state hours before his death."
  clue_05 — "Physically connects Solís to the forced entry point: her fingerprints
             on the crowbar used to lever the rear window establish she entered
             through the back, not the locked front door."
  clue_06 — "Blade geometry matches the wound channel exactly, confirming the
             recovered knife as the specific murder weapon rather than an incidental
             object found at the scene."
  clue_07 — "Solís's initials on the blade directly attribute ownership of the
             murder weapon to her, placing the instrument of death in her possession
             before the killing."
  clue_S2 — "Solís's own confession confirms she forced entry, struck Hale when he
             threatened to call the police, and fled — completing the chain from
             motive through opportunity to the act."
  clue_08 — "Finn Dray's alibi — corroborated service at the main house until past
             midnight — rules him out entirely and eliminates the only other person
             with proximate access to the property."

  PROCESS EXPLANATION:
  Mara Solís (char_mara), the estate's head groundskeeper, killed Victor Hale with
  her own fixed-blade hunting knife (means_blade) in the locked garden shed between
  ten at night and midnight. Facing demolition of her cottage under Hale's planned
  sale, she forced the rear window with a crowbar and struck him when he threatened
  to call the police. Her fingerprints were on the crowbar, her initials were on the
  blade, and her confession confirmed the act. Finn Dray (char_finn) is exonerated —
  he was serving guests until past midnight and had no motive.

DO NOT COMMIT EARLY. An incomplete investigation is the most common failure mode.
There are clues you cannot see without using present — if you have not presented
your items and contradictions to every character, you are missing evidence.

input.content:
You are an expert detective solving a murder mystery. Your goal is a
complete, evidence-backed solution — not a fast one. Commit only when you have exhausted
the evidence available to you.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
NAVIGATION — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Movement is EXIT-BASED. You can ONLY call move_to() with a location ID that
appears in your CURRENT EXITS list. There is no teleportation.

To reach a room that is not in your current exits, you must pass through
intermediate rooms. BEFORE calling move_to(), always ask yourself:
  "Is my destination in my current exits list?"
  • YES → call move_to(destination).
  • NO  → find a path: which room you've already visited has an exit to my
           destination? Move there first, then continue.

BUILD A MENTAL MAP as you explore. After every move, record:
  loc_X  exits → [loc_A, loc_B, loc_C]

EXAMPLE — navigating two hops:
  You are at loc_harbor (exits: loc_alley).
  You want loc_office, but it's not in your current exits.
  You recall that loc_alley has exits including loc_office.
  → move_to("loc_alley") first, then move_to("loc_office").

If you cannot remember which room connects to your destination, call
get_investigation_summary() — it shows your current location and you can
reason from your recorded map. Do NOT try to move_to() a location that is
not in your current exits; it will fail and waste your budget.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
CHARACTER TRACKING — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Characters are stationary at their home locations (unless a special event moves
them). You must VISIT a location to discover who is there.

After each move, record the characters present:
  loc_X → characters: [char_finn, char_mara]
  loc_Y → characters: [char_silas]

Use the briefing cast list to track who you have NOT yet found. If a character
has not appeared anywhere you've visited, they are waiting in an unvisited room.
Do not assume a room is empty until you have been there and seen "nobody".

Characters present are listed in the result of every move_to() call — read that
list carefully and update your mental register immediately.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
GAME MECHANICS — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ACTIONS (each costs 1):
  move_to(location_id)                — travel to an adjacent room
  search_location()                   — collect physical evidence from the current room
  interview_character(character_id)   — hear a character's freely offered testimony
  present_to_character(character_id, ref_id) — show an item or clue to unlock hidden testimony

TWO LAYERS OF CHARACTER TESTIMONY:
  Layer 1 — FREELY OFFERED: every character has clues they give when interviewed.
             These are always the same. Interviewing twice gives no new information.
  Layer 2 — GUARDED: every character may have additional clues they only reveal
             when shown the right evidence. You CANNOT get guarded testimony
             through interview alone. You MUST use present_to_character.

THE PRESENT ACTION IS CRITICAL:
  • If you found an ITEM while searching (shown as "item added to inventory"),
    visit every character you've met and present that item to them.
  • If a character LIED or their story CONTRADICTS a clue you collected,
    present the contradicting clue to them — they may break and reveal the truth.
  • A present that doesn't unlock anything costs 1 action but you learn the
    combination didn't work. Keep trying other items/clues on other characters.
  • Some characters require being confronted in sequence: a partial admission
    unlocked by one present may need a second present with different evidence
    before the full truth comes out.

LOCKED LOCATIONS:
  • Some rooms are locked and show in your exits list but cannot be entered.
  • To unlock them, find the key item (by searching other locations) and carry it.
  • Once the item is in your inventory the locked room becomes accessible automatically.

SEARCH IS MANDATORY:
  • Evidence only appears via search_location(). A room with searchable=True
    that you haven't searched may contain the most important clue in the case.
  • Search every room you visit that shows searchable=True. No exceptions.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
INVESTIGATION PROTOCOL — FOLLOW THIS ORDER
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PHASE 1 — SWEEP (visit and collect everything)
  For every location in the briefing:
    a. Plan your path using your mental map — navigate hop by hop through exits.
    b. If searchable=True → search_location().
    c. Interview every character present (check "Characters here" in move result).
    d. Note exits, update your map, note any items gained and locked exits.

PHASE 2 — UNLOCK (present evidence to every character)
  For each item in your inventory:
    → Navigate to every character you've met and present it to them.
  For each clue that seems to contradict a character's story:
    → Navigate to that character and present the contradicting clue.
  For any character whose response to a present hinted at more ("that proves
  nothing", "ask someone else", a partial admission):
    → Try presenting additional evidence to them — they likely have a second
       guarded clue waiting to be unlocked.

PHASE 3 — LOCKED ROOMS (once you have items)
  Revisit any location that was locked earlier. Search it. Interview characters inside.

PHASE 4 — CROSS-CHECK
  Call get_investigation_summary() and confirm:
    ✓ You have visited every location in the briefing list.
    ✓ You have searched every searchable location.
    ✓ You have found and interviewed EVERY character from the cast list.
    ✓ You have presented every inventory item to every relevant character.
    ✓ You have confronted characters whose story contradicts collected clues.
  If any box is unchecked and you have budget, plan a path and go fill the gap.

PHASE 5 — COMMIT (write your handoff first)
  Only after completing Phases 1–4, or if you have fewer than 10 actions remaining.

  Before stopping, write a complete handoff document so the Output Parser can extract
  your CommitDecision cleanly. Organise it however makes sense to you — the only
  requirement is that all of the following are present and unambiguous:

    1. The culprit ID and means ID, clearly labelled.
    2. One evidence note per clue you collected — every clue, not just the ones
       on the critical path. Alibis, contradictions, and exonerating evidence all
       count. The clue_id and a concise explanation of what it proves (~120 words).
       Group or order them however is logical (chronological, by type, by character — your call).
    3. The full process explanation as a prose narrative (no clue IDs).

  Think of this as a case file handed to a colleague who hasn't followed your
  investigation — everything they need to verify and record your answer should
  be on the page, nothing more.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
HOW YOUR ANSWER IS SCORED
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Your CommitDecision has four fields:
  culprit_id         — 0.35 points if correct
  means_id           — 0.10 points if correct
  evidence_notes     — up to 0.30 points (scored per note):
    One dict per key clue: {"clue_id": "...", "note": "..."}.
    Each note is compared by embedding to the reference for that clue.
    Submit notes for every clue you found — non-key clues are silently ignored.
    Notes on clue IDs you never found, or duplicate IDs, are penalised as wrong.
    Missing a key clue scores 0 — no extra penalty. Target ~120 words per note.
  process_explanation — up to 0.15 points:
    Short holistic narrative: who did it, how, and why. NO clue IDs.
    Target ~120 words. Scored by embedding similarity (x³ curve — aim for
    strong conceptual alignment with the hidden reference narrative).

WRITING evidence_notes — STYLE GUIDE:

Write a note for EVERY clue you collected during the investigation — not just
the ones that prove the culprit. Include alibis, contradictions, exonerating
clues, and anything that ruled out a suspect. If you found it, note it.
Non-key clues are silently ignored by the scorer — they don't hurt you.
What DOES hurt you: submitting a clue_id you never actually found, or
submitting the same clue_id twice. Don't fabricate or duplicate.

Use the clue_id exactly as it appears in your investigation
(e.g. "clue_04", "clue_M2", "clue_S1").

Each note must explain WHAT THE CLUE PROVES IN CONTEXT — not just describe
what it says. Lead with the evidentiary significance; use an em-dash to extend
into its role in the chain if needed:
  Good: "Establishes the killer placed himself at the scene by his own signed
         transfer record — providing both a pretext to draw the victim there and
         documentary proof of his presence."
  Bad:  "The transfer record logs a midnight delivery."

Target ~120 words per note. Do not include clue IDs inside the note text.

WRITING THE process_explanation — STYLE GUIDE:

Write in flowing prose paragraphs, not bullet points or numbered lists.
Do NOT include any clue IDs (e.g. clue_04, clue_M2). Reference evidence by
description: "the delivery manifest", "the witness on the dock", "his confession".

STRUCTURE (follow this order):

  Paragraph 1 — VERDICT:
    Open with one sentence naming the culprit (ID + display name + role) and the
    means (ID + display name), plus the location and time window of the killing.
    E.g.: "The killer is [Name] ([char_*]), the station's [role], who killed
    [Victim] with [means display name] ([means_*]) at [place] between [times]."

  Paragraphs 2–N — CASE NARRATIVE (weave motive, setup, and execution together):
    Tell the story in roughly chronological order: motive → method → opportunity
    → physical evidence → witness → confession. Reference evidence by description,
    not clue ID. Each paragraph should advance the chain of events.

  Final paragraph — EXONERATION:
    Explicitly clear every other suspect. Name each one and state what rules them
    out (alibi, settled motive, physical impossibility) — in plain prose, no IDs.

STYLE RULES:
  • Write in the third person, past tense, declarative sentences.
  • Be specific: name roles, locations, times, and relationships.
  • Do not use headers, dashes, or numbered points inside the explanation.
  • Target ~120 words, hard cap 140. Verbosity penalty applies beyond that.

EXAMPLE OUTPUT (fictional seed — shows correct style for both sections):

  EVIDENCE NOTES:
  clue_01 — "Establishes the eviction notice placed Solís under imminent threat of
             losing her home three days before the murder — providing the concrete
             personal motive that connects her to the killing."
  clue_02 — "Records a direct verbal confrontation between Solís and Hale the
             afternoon of the murder, in which she threatened him — placing her in
             an antagonistic state hours before his death."
  clue_05 — "Physically connects Solís to the forced entry point: her fingerprints
             on the crowbar used to lever the rear window establish she entered
             through the back, not the locked front door."
  clue_06 — "Blade geometry matches the wound channel exactly, confirming the
             recovered knife as the specific murder weapon rather than an incidental
             object found at the scene."
  clue_07 — "Solís's initials on the blade directly attribute ownership of the
             murder weapon to her, placing the instrument of death in her possession
             before the killing."
  clue_S2 — "Solís's own confession confirms she forced entry, struck Hale when he
             threatened to call the police, and fled — completing the chain from
             motive through opportunity to the act."
  clue_08 — "Finn Dray's alibi — corroborated service at the main house until past
             midnight — rules him out entirely and eliminates the only other person
             with proximate access to the property."

  PROCESS EXPLANATION:
  Mara Solís (char_mara), the estate's head groundskeeper, killed Victor Hale with
  her own fixed-blade hunting knife (means_blade) in the locked garden shed between
  ten at night and midnight. Facing demolition of her cottage under Hale's planned
  sale, she forced the rear window with a crowbar and struck him when he threatened
  to call the police. Her fingerprints were on the crowbar, her initials were on the
  blade, and her confession confirmed the act. Finn Dray (char_finn) is exonerated —
  he was serving guests until past midnight and had no motive.

DO NOT COMMIT EARLY. An incomplete investigation is the most common failure mode.
There are clues you cannot see without using present — if you have not presented
your items and contradictions to every character, you are missing evidence.

You are an expert detective solving a murder mystery. Your goal is a
complete, evidence-backed solution — not a fast one. Commit only when you have exhausted
the evidence available to you.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
NAVIGATION — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Movement is EXIT-BASED. You can ONLY call move_to() with a location ID that
appears in your CURRENT EXITS list. There is no teleportation.

To reach a room that is not in your current exits, you must pass through
intermediate rooms. BEFORE calling move_to(), always ask yourself:
  "Is my destination in my current exits list?"
  • YES → call move_to(destination).
  • NO  → find a path: which room you've already visited has an exit to my
           destination? Move there first, then continue.

BUILD A MENTAL MAP as you explore. After every move, record:
  loc_X  exits → [loc_A, loc_B, loc_C]

EXAMPLE — navigating two hops:
  You are at loc_harbor (exits: loc_alley).
  You want loc_office, but it's not in your current exits.
  You recall that loc_alley has exits including loc_office.
  → move_to("loc_alley") first, then move_to("loc_office").

If you cannot remember which room connects to your destination, call
get_investigation_summary() — it shows your current location and you can
reason from your recorded map. Do NOT try to move_to() a location that is
not in your current exits; it will fail and waste your budget.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
CHARACTER TRACKING — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Characters are stationary at their home locations (unless a special event moves
them). You must VISIT a location to discover who is there.

After each move, record the characters present:
  loc_X → characters: [char_finn, char_mara]
  loc_Y → characters: [char_silas]

Use the briefing cast list to track who you have NOT yet found. If a character
has not appeared anywhere you've visited, they are waiting in an unvisited room.
Do not assume a room is empty until you have been there and seen "nobody".

Characters present are listed in the result of every move_to() call — read that
list carefully and update your mental register immediately.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
GAME MECHANICS — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ACTIONS (each costs 1):
  move_to(location_id)                — travel to an adjacent room
  search_location()                   — collect physical evidence from the current room
  interview_character(character_id)   — hear a character's freely offered testimony
  present_to_character(character_id, ref_id) — show an item or clue to unlock hidden testimony

TWO LAYERS OF CHARACTER TESTIMONY:
  Layer 1 — FREELY OFFERED: every character has clues they give when interviewed.
             These are always the same. Interviewing twice gives no new information.
  Layer 2 — GUARDED: every character may have additional clues they only reveal
             when shown the right evidence. You CANNOT get guarded testimony
             through interview alone. You MUST use present_to_character.

THE PRESENT ACTION IS CRITICAL:
  • If you found an ITEM while searching (shown as "item added to inventory"),
    visit every character you've met and present that item to them.
  • If a character LIED or their story CONTRADICTS a clue you collected,
    present the contradicting clue to them — they may break and reveal the truth.
  • A present that doesn't unlock anything costs 1 action but you learn the
    combination didn't work. Keep trying other items/clues on other characters.
  • Some characters require being confronted in sequence: a partial admission
    unlocked by one present may need a second present with different evidence
    before the full truth comes out.

LOCKED LOCATIONS:
  • Some rooms are locked and show in your exits list but cannot be entered.
  • To unlock them, find the key item (by searching other locations) and carry it.
  • Once the item is in your inventory the locked room becomes accessible automatically.

SEARCH IS MANDATORY:
  • Evidence only appears via search_location(). A room with searchable=True
    that you haven't searched may contain the most important clue in the case.
  • Search every room you visit that shows searchable=True. No exceptions.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
INVESTIGATION PROTOCOL — FOLLOW THIS ORDER
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PHASE 1 — SWEEP (visit and collect everything)
  For every location in the briefing:
    a. Plan your path using your mental map — navigate hop by hop through exits.
    b. If searchable=True → search_location().
    c. Interview every character present (check "Characters here" in move result).
    d. Note exits, update your map, note any items gained and locked exits.

PHASE 2 — UNLOCK (present evidence to every character)
  For each item in your inventory:
    → Navigate to every character you've met and present it to them.
  For each clue that seems to contradict a character's story:
    → Navigate to that character and present the contradicting clue.
  For any character whose response to a present hinted at more ("that proves
  nothing", "ask someone else", a partial admission):
    → Try presenting additional evidence to them — they likely have a second
       guarded clue waiting to be unlocked.

PHASE 3 — LOCKED ROOMS (once you have items)
  Revisit any location that was locked earlier. Search it. Interview characters inside.

PHASE 4 — CROSS-CHECK
  Call get_investigation_summary() and confirm:
    ✓ You have visited every location in the briefing list.
    ✓ You have searched every searchable location.
    ✓ You have found and interviewed EVERY character from the cast list.
    ✓ You have presented every inventory item to every relevant character.
    ✓ You have confronted characters whose story contradicts collected clues.
  If any box is unchecked and you have budget, plan a path and go fill the gap.

PHASE 5 — COMMIT (write your handoff first)
  Only after completing Phases 1–4, or if you have fewer than 10 actions remaining.

  Before stopping, write a complete handoff document so the Output Parser can extract
  your CommitDecision cleanly. Organise it however makes sense to you — the only
  requirement is that all of the following are present and unambiguous:

    1. The culprit ID and means ID, clearly labelled.
    2. One evidence note per clue you collected — every clue, not just the ones
       on the critical path. Alibis, contradictions, and exonerating evidence all
       count. The clue_id and a concise explanation of what it proves (~120 words).
       Group or order them however is logical (chronological, by type, by character — your call).
    3. The full process explanation as a prose narrative (no clue IDs).

  Think of this as a case file handed to a colleague who hasn't followed your
  investigation — everything they need to verify and record your answer should
  be on the page, nothing more.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
HOW YOUR ANSWER IS SCORED
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Your CommitDecision has four fields:
  culprit_id         — 0.35 points if correct
  means_id           — 0.10 points if correct
  evidence_notes     — up to 0.30 points (scored per note):
    One dict per key clue: {"clue_id": "...", "note": "..."}.
    Each note is compared by embedding to the reference for that clue.
    Submit notes for every clue you found — non-key clues are silently ignored.
    Notes on clue IDs you never found, or duplicate IDs, are penalised as wrong.
    Missing a key clue scores 0 — no extra penalty. Target ~120 words per note.
  process_explanation — up to 0.15 points:
    Short holistic narrative: who did it, how, and why. NO clue IDs.
    Target ~120 words. Scored by embedding similarity (x³ curve — aim for
    strong conceptual alignment with the hidden reference narrative).

WRITING evidence_notes — STYLE GUIDE:

Write a note for EVERY clue you collected during the investigation — not just
the ones that prove the culprit. Include alibis, contradictions, exonerating
clues, and anything that ruled out a suspect. If you found it, note it.
Non-key clues are silently ignored by the scorer — they don't hurt you.
What DOES hurt you: submitting a clue_id you never actually found, or
submitting the same clue_id twice. Don't fabricate or duplicate.

Use the clue_id exactly as it appears in your investigation
(e.g. "clue_04", "clue_M2", "clue_S1").

Each note must explain WHAT THE CLUE PROVES IN CONTEXT — not just describe
what it says. Lead with the evidentiary significance; use an em-dash to extend
into its role in the chain if needed:
  Good: "Establishes the killer placed himself at the scene by his own signed
         transfer record — providing both a pretext to draw the victim there and
         documentary proof of his presence."
  Bad:  "The transfer record logs a midnight delivery."

Target ~120 words per note. Do not include clue IDs inside the note text.

WRITING THE process_explanation — STYLE GUIDE:

Write in flowing prose paragraphs, not bullet points or numbered lists.
Do NOT include any clue IDs (e.g. clue_04, clue_M2). Reference evidence by
description: "the delivery manifest", "the witness on the dock", "his confession".

STRUCTURE (follow this order):

  Paragraph 1 — VERDICT:
    Open with one sentence naming the culprit (ID + display name + role) and the
    means (ID + display name), plus the location and time window of the killing.
    E.g.: "The killer is [Name] ([char_*]), the station's [role], who killed
    [Victim] with [means display name] ([means_*]) at [place] between [times]."

  Paragraphs 2–N — CASE NARRATIVE (weave motive, setup, and execution together):
    Tell the story in roughly chronological order: motive → method → opportunity
    → physical evidence → witness → confession. Reference evidence by description,
    not clue ID. Each paragraph should advance the chain of events.

  Final paragraph — EXONERATION:
    Explicitly clear every other suspect. Name each one and state what rules them
    out (alibi, settled motive, physical impossibility) — in plain prose, no IDs.

STYLE RULES:
  • Write in the third person, past tense, declarative sentences.
  • Be specific: name roles, locations, times, and relationships.
  • Do not use headers, dashes, or numbered points inside the explanation.
  • Target ~120 words, hard cap 140. Verbosity penalty applies beyond that.

EXAMPLE OUTPUT (fictional seed — shows correct style for both sections):

  EVIDENCE NOTES:
  clue_01 — "Establishes the eviction notice placed Solís under imminent threat of
             losing her home three days before the murder — providing the concrete
             personal motive that connects her to the killing."
  clue_02 — "Records a direct verbal confrontation between Solís and Hale the
             afternoon of the murder, in which she threatened him — placing her in
             an antagonistic state hours before his death."
  clue_05 — "Physically connects Solís to the forced entry point: her fingerprints
             on the crowbar used to lever the rear window establish she entered
             through the back, not the locked front door."
  clue_06 — "Blade geometry matches the wound channel exactly, confirming the
             recovered knife as the specific murder weapon rather than an incidental
             object found at the scene."
  clue_07 — "Solís's initials on the blade directly attribute ownership of the
             murder weapon to her, placing the instrument of death in her possession
             before the killing."
  clue_S2 — "Solís's own confession confirms she forced entry, struck Hale when he
             threatened to call the police, and fled — completing the chain from
             motive through opportunity to the act."
  clue_08 — "Finn Dray's alibi — corroborated service at the main house until past
             midnight — rules him out entirely and eliminates the only other person
             with proximate access to the property."

  PROCESS EXPLANATION:
  Mara Solís (char_mara), the estate's head groundskeeper, killed Victor Hale with
  her own fixed-blade hunting knife (means_blade) in the locked garden shed between
  ten at night and midnight. Facing demolition of her cottage under Hale's planned
  sale, she forced the rear window with a crowbar and struck him when he threatened
  to call the police. Her fingerprints were on the crowbar, her initials were on the
  blade, and her confession confirmed the act. Finn Dray (char_finn) is exonerated —
  he was serving guests until past midnight and had no motive.

DO NOT COMMIT EARLY. An incomplete investigation is the most common failure mode.
There are clues you cannot see without using present — if you have not presented
your items and contradictions to every character, you are missing evidence.

═══ CASE BRIEFING ═══

input.content:
You are an expert detective solving a murder mystery. Your goal is a
complete, evidence-backed solution — not a fast one. Commit only when you have exhausted
the evidence available to you.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
NAVIGATION — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Movement is EXIT-BASED. You can ONLY call move_to() with a location ID that
appears in your CURRENT EXITS list. There is no teleportation.

To reach a room that is not in your current exits, you must pass through
intermediate rooms. BEFORE calling move_to(), always ask yourself:
  "Is my destination in my current exits list?"
  • YES → call move_to(destination).
  • NO  → find a path: which room you've already visited has an exit to my
           destination? Move there first, then continue.

BUILD A MENTAL MAP as you explore. After every move, record:
  loc_X  exits → [loc_A, loc_B, loc_C]

EXAMPLE — navigating two hops:
  You are at loc_harbor (exits: loc_alley).
  You want loc_office, but it's not in your current exits.
  You recall that loc_alley has exits including loc_office.
  → move_to("loc_alley") first, then move_to("loc_office").

If you cannot remember which room connects to your destination, call
get_investigation_summary() — it shows your current location and you can
reason from your recorded map. Do NOT try to move_to() a location that is
not in your current exits; it will fail and waste your budget.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
CHARACTER TRACKING — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Characters are stationary at their home locations (unless a special event moves
them). You must VISIT a location to discover who is there.

After each move, record the characters present:
  loc_X → characters: [char_finn, char_mara]
  loc_Y → characters: [char_silas]

Use the briefing cast list to track who you have NOT yet found. If a character
has not appeared anywhere you've visited, they are waiting in an unvisited room.
Do not assume a room is empty until you have been there and seen "nobody".

Characters present are listed in the result of every move_to() call — read that
list carefully and update your mental register immediately.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
GAME MECHANICS — READ CAREFULLY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ACTIONS (each costs 1):
  move_to(location_id)                — travel to an adjacent room
  search_location()                   — collect physical evidence from the current room
  interview_character(character_id)   — hear a character's freely offered testimony
  present_to_character(character_id, ref_id) — show an item or clue to unlock hidden testimony

TWO LAYERS OF CHARACTER TESTIMONY:
  Layer 1 — FREELY OFFERED: every character has clues they give when interviewed.
             These are always the same. Interviewing twice gives no new information.
  Layer 2 — GUARDED: every character may have additional clues they only reveal
             when shown the right evidence. You CANNOT get guarded testimony
             through interview alone. You MUST use present_to_character.

THE PRESENT ACTION IS CRITICAL:
  • If you found an ITEM while searching (shown as "item added to inventory"),
    visit every character you've met and present that item to them.
  • If a character LIED or their story CONTRADICTS a clue you collected,
    present the contradicting clue to them — they may break and reveal the truth.
  • A present that doesn't unlock anything costs 1 action but you learn the
    combination didn't work. Keep trying other items/clues on other characters.
  • Some characters require being confronted in sequence: a partial admission
    unlocked by one present may need a second present with different evidence
    before the full truth comes out.

LOCKED LOCATIONS:
  • Some rooms are locked and show in your exits list but cannot be entered.
  • To unlock them, find the key item (by searching other locations) and carry it.
  • Once the item is in your inventory the locked room becomes accessible automatically.

SEARCH IS MANDATORY:
  • Evidence only appears via search_location(). A room with searchable=True
    that you haven't searched may contain the most important clue in the case.
  • Search every room you visit that shows searchable=True. No exceptions.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
INVESTIGATION PROTOCOL — FOLLOW THIS ORDER
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PHASE 1 — SWEEP (visit and collect everything)
  For every location in the briefing:
    a. Plan your path using your mental map — navigate hop by hop through exits.
    b. If searchable=True → search_location().
    c. Interview every character present (check "Characters here" in move result).
    d. Note exits, update your map, note any items gained and locked exits.

PHASE 2 — UNLOCK (present evidence to every character)
  For each item in your inventory:
    → Navigate to every character you've met and present it to them.
  For each clue that seems to contradict a character's story:
    → Navigate to that character and present the contradicting clue.
  For any character whose response to a present hinted at more ("that proves
  nothing", "ask someone else", a partial admission):
    → Try presenting additional evidence to them — they likely have a second
       guarded clue waiting to be unlocked.

PHASE 3 — LOCKED ROOMS (once you have items)
  Revisit any location that was locked earlier. Search it. Interview characters inside.

PHASE 4 — CROSS-CHECK
  Call get_investigation_summary() and confirm:
    ✓ You have visited every location in the briefing list.
    ✓ You have searched every searchable location.
    ✓ You have found and interviewed EVERY character from the cast list.
    ✓ You have presented every inventory item to every relevant character.
    ✓ You have confronted characters whose story contradicts collected clues.
  If any box is unchecked and you have budget, plan a path and go fill the gap.

PHASE 5 — COMMIT (write your handoff first)
  Only after completing Phases 1–4, or if you have fewer than 10 actions remaining.

  Before stopping, write a complete handoff document so the Output Parser can extract
  your CommitDecision cleanly. Organise it however makes sense to you — the only
  requirement is that all of the following are present and unambiguous:

    1. The culprit ID and means ID, clearly labelled.
    2. One evidence note per clue you collected — every clue, not just the ones
       on the critical path. Alibis, contradictions, and exonerating evidence all
       count. The clue_id and a concise explanation of what it proves (~120 words).
       Group or order them however is logical (chronological, by type, by character — your call).
    3. The full process explanation as a prose narrative (no clue IDs).

  Think of this as a case file handed to a colleague who hasn't followed your
  investigation — everything they need to verify and record your answer should
  be on the page, nothing more.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
HOW YOUR ANSWER IS SCORED
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Your CommitDecision has four fields:
  culprit_id         — 0.35 points if correct
  means_id           — 0.10 points if correct
  evidence_notes     — up to 0.30 points (scored per note):
    One dict per key clue: {"clue_id": "...", "note": "..."}.
    Each note is compared by embedding to the reference for that clue.
    Submit notes for every clue you found — non-key clues are silently ignored.
    Notes on clue IDs you never found, or duplicate IDs, are penalised as wrong.
    Missing a key clue scores 0 — no extra penalty. Target ~120 words per note.
  process_explanation — up to 0.15 points:
    Short holistic narrative: who did it, how, and why. NO clue IDs.
    Target ~120 words. Scored by embedding similarity (x³ curve — aim for
    strong conceptual alignment with the hidden reference narrative).

WRITING evidence_notes — STYLE GUIDE:

Write a note for EVERY clue you collected during the investigation — not just
the ones that prove the culprit. Include alibis, contradictions, exonerating
clues, and anything that ruled out a suspect. If you found it, note it.
Non-key clues are silently ignored by the scorer — they don't hurt you.
What DOES hurt you: submitting a clue_id you never actually found, or
submitting the same clue_id twice. Don't fabricate or duplicate.

Use the clue_id exactly as it appears in your investigation
(e.g. "clue_04", "clue_M2", "clue_S1").

Each note must explain WHAT THE CLUE PROVES IN CONTEXT — not just describe
what it says. Lead with the evidentiary significance; use an em-dash to extend
into its role in the chain if needed:
  Good: "Establishes the killer placed himself at the scene by his own signed
         transfer record — providing both a pretext to draw the victim there and
         documentary proof of his presence."
  Bad:  "The transfer record logs a midnight delivery."

Target ~120 words per note. Do not include clue IDs inside the note text.

WRITING THE process_explanation — STYLE GUIDE:

Write in flowing prose paragraphs, not bullet points or numbered lists.
Do NOT include any clue IDs (e.g. clue_04, clue_M2). Reference evidence by
description: "the delivery manifest", "the witness on the dock", "his confession".

STRUCTURE (follow this order):

  Paragraph 1 — VERDICT:
    Open with one sentence naming the culprit (ID + display name + role) and the
    means (ID + display name), plus the location and time window of the killing.
    E.g.: "The killer is [Name] ([char_*]), the station's [role], who killed
    [Victim] with [means display name] ([means_*]) at [place] between [times]."

  Paragraphs 2–N — CASE NARRATIVE (weave motive, setup, and execution together):
    Tell the story in roughly chronological order: motive → method → opportunity
    → physical evidence → witness → confession. Reference evidence by description,
    not clue ID. Each paragraph should advance the chain of events.

  Final paragraph — EXONERATION:
    Explicitly clear every other suspect. Name each one and state what rules them
    out (alibi, settled motive, physical impossibility) — in plain prose, no IDs.

STYLE RULES:
  • Write in the third person, past tense, declarative sentences.
  • Be specific: name roles, locations, times, and relationships.
  • Do not use headers, dashes, or numbered points inside the explanation.
  • Target ~120 words, hard cap 140. Verbosity penalty applies beyond that.

EXAMPLE OUTPUT (fictional seed — shows correct style for both sections):

  EVIDENCE NOTES:
  clue_01 — "Establishes the eviction notice placed Solís under imminent threat of
             losing her home three days before the murder — providing the concrete
             personal motive that connects her to the killing."
  clue_02 — "Records a direct verbal confrontation between Solís and Hale the
             afternoon of the murder, in which she threatened him — placing her in
             an antagonistic state hours before his death."
  clue_05 — "Physically connects Solís to the forced entry point: her fingerprints
             on the crowbar used to lever the rear window establish she entered
             through the back, not the locked front door."
  clue_06 — "Blade geometry matches the wound channel exactly, confirming the
             recovered knife as the specific murder weapon rather than an incidental
             object found at the scene."
  clue_07 — "Solís's initials on the blade directly attribute ownership of the
             murder weapon to her, placing the instrument of death in her possession
             before the killing."
  clue_S2 — "Solís's own confession confirms she forced entry, struck Hale when he
             threatened to call the police, and fled — completing the chain from
             motive through opportunity to the act."
  clue_08 — "Finn Dray's alibi — corroborated service at the main house until past
             midnight — rules him out entirely and eliminates the only other person
             with proximate access to the property."

  PROCESS EXPLANATION:
  Mara Solís (char_mara), the estate's head groundskeeper, killed Victor Hale with
  her own fixed-blade hunting knife (means_blade) in the locked garden shed between
  ten at night and midnight. Facing demolition of her cottage under Hale's planned
  sale, she forced the rear window with a crowbar and struck him when he threatened
  to call the police. Her fingerprints were on the crowbar, her initials were on the
  blade, and her confession confirmed the act. Finn Dray (char_finn) is exonerated —
  he was serving guests until past midnight and had no motive.

DO NOT COMMIT EARLY. An incomplete investigation is the most common failure mode.

output.content:
EVIDENCE NOTES:
clue_01 — The victim is Aldric Marsh, harbor merchant, age 47. This identification anchors the case: Marsh’s role as a dockside entrepreneur means he moved valuable goods through the Riverside and Harbor districts, placing him in proximity to both legitimate trade and illicit cargo networks. Establishing his occupation and age helps assess motive and opportunity, since a figure involved in risky shipments would have both allies and enemies among those who benefit from the trade or from underhanded deals. It also frames the time window of the crime: someone who knew Marsh’s routines could exploit his confidence around dawn when ships were switching cargo. The detail grounds the investigation in a concrete social and economic position that links suspect networks to the crime scene.

clue_02 — The cause of death is a single deep blade wound to the chest, consistent with a fixed-blade knife. This physical clue narrows the mechanism of the murder and points to a weapon capable of delivering a decisive, fatal stab. In practical terms, it excludes more leisurely methods (poison, rope as strangulation) and ties the crime to a hands-on assault. The rigidity of a fixed blade also implies premeditation or a direct confrontation rather than a quick, accidental contact. With a blade as the murder instrument, the killer’s access to a personal knife or a weapon readily available at the docks becomes a plausible line of inquiry, linking weapon choice to a likely local actor.

clue_03 — Time of death is estimated between midnight and 2 a.m., based on body temperature. This narrows the critical window for the crime and constrains suspects to those active or in proximity during those hours. It harmonizes with other time-based clues, such as witness reports or ledger events occurring around that period. In practical terms, any alibi or plan tied to the hours around midnight must be tested against Marsh’s routine and the suspects’ movements: who could be at the docks at that late hour, who had access to the Riverside Warehouse, and who stood to gain from Marsh’s death if a midnight shipment mask was involved?

clue_F1 — Deputy Finn found the body at first light near Pier 3; Marsh had been there for a few hours and the blood had darkened. This corroborates the death window inferred from Marsh’s body temperature and places the scene at Pier 3 as the primary site of death. Finn’s observation that the body had been there for hours supports a late-night arrival of Marsh or a late-night confrontation there. The location near Pier 3 also aligns with the docks’ transport routes, suggesting someone familiar with maritime traffic could have approached Marsh during a quiet moment at dawn. This testimony helps calibrate the narrative timeline and narrows the geography of the crime.

clue_F2 — Finn notes Marsh had a reputation for clean dealings; dock hands criticized him for underpaying. This establishes Marsh’s social and economic context: he was a mercantile figure whose business practices could create friction with workers, rivals, or associates who resented him. While not a direct link to the killer, it broadens the field of possible suspects by highlighting motive-based tensions tied to the docks’ labor and shipping economy. It also seeds potential grievances that could be exploited by someone aiming to silence Marsh to protect a clandestine operation or illegal cargo. The social backdrop matters to motive construction.

clue_04 — The Shipping Ledger shows a midnight delivery of bonded cargo to the riverside warehouse, authorised and signed for by the harbormaster’s deputy, Silas Vane. This is a pivotal connection: it directly ties Silas to a midnight shipment at the riverside warehouse, placing him at the center of both Marsh’s death window and the illegal cargo dynamic. The fact that the delivery was bonded and officially signed for by Silas suggests either his control over or complicity within illicit operations, creating a powerful motive to silence Marsh if the latter threatened exposure. This clue shifts the investigation toward Silas as a principal actor with access to the critical locations involved in the crime.

clue_05 — The handwriting on the ledger matches Silas Vane’s known signature on the front page. This strengthens the inference that Silas authored or authorized the midnight delivery entry, elevating him from a potential collaborator to a person with direct involvement in the illicit cargo operation. A signature match is a high-confidence link that translates into a concrete, documentary proof of Silas’s control over the transaction recorded in the ledger. That control would afford him both motive and opportunity to confront Marsh if Marsh threatened to expose the operation. The handwriting match consolidates the case against Silas.

clue_M1 — Mara saw a tall man with a pronounced limp crossing toward the warehouse gates around midnight; he moved quickly, as if trying not to be seen. This is a crucial eyewitness fragment that aligns with the surveillance of Silas’s characteristics and the timing of midnight activity near the Riverside Warehouse. The description of a limp provides a distinctive physical feature that Mara could plausibly identify later, tying the observed individual to someone known to us—most strongly Silas, given his later associations with the ledger. The sighting places a person matching Silas’s profile at the threshold of the crime scene during Marsh’s late-night peril, reinforcing the likelihood of a confrontation around the warehouse perimeter.

clue_M2 — The ledger Mara shows is Silas’s own ledger for private cargo runs; Mara also identifies the limp as Silas and declares it was him. This is especially damning: it directly links Silas to the private cargo network through his own records and confirms what Mara observed about the limp. It transforms a vague eyewitness memory into a documentary-alibi-weakening fact: Silas’s private ledger proves his involvement in illicit shipments, and Mara’s identification of him as the limping man at the warehouse gates ties him physically to the midnight crossing. This combination of documentary and eyewitness evidence forms a turning point in the investigation.

clue_W1 — A search warrant signed by the magistrate authorized entry to bonded warehouses on the dock district. This credential shows there was official government permission to inspect key locations, and it legitimizes the potential access Silas would have had to the Riverside Warehouse and its ledgers. It also frames the ability of a law-enforcement-adjacent actor to move within the warehouses to secure or conceal contraband shipments. The warrant’s existence corroborates the plausibility of the midnight delivery trace and supports the possibility that those with access to the warehouses—like Silas—could orchestrate or obstruct exposure. It underlines procedural context rather than direct guilt, but it strengthens the chain of custody around the relevant sites.

clue_S1 — Silas Vane’s claim: he was at the office finishing month-end accounts all evening, left around ten, and went straight home; he denies knowledge of any midnight delivery. This is a guarded claim that must be weighed against other converging clues. The contradiction with the ledger showing a midnight shipment authorized by Silas casts doubt on his alibi and raises questions about his presence or control over illicit operations at or near the Riverside Warehouse during the covered window. The alibi creates a narrative tension: either he is lying or the ledger is forged or misused. It’s a critical node in the investigation, demanding further corroboration or contradiction through other witnesses or documents.

PROCESS EXPLANATION:
The killer is Silas Vane (char_silas), Harbormaster’s deputy, who used a blade to kill Aldric Marsh at the Riverside Warehouse area near the docks, in the midnight-to-dawn window. The evidence points to a carefully staged confrontation tied to a clandestine cargo scheme: a ledger of midnight bonded deliveries shows Silas authorised and recorded shipments to the riverside warehouse, and the handwriting on the ledger matches Silas’s own signature, indicating his control over the operation. Mara’s testimony about a limping man crossing toward the warehouse at midnight, reinforced by her identification of Silas as the limping figure, links him physically to the scene. Marsh’s body bears a fatal blade wound, consistent with a personal knife, aligning with the confrontation rather than a distant plot. The discovery of Silas’s private ledger and his contested alibi further erode any defense that he was merely a bystander.

The motive centers on silencing a potential exposé of the illicit cargo run; Marsh’s death could be the result of his threat to reveal the operation. The scene was prepared to resemble a random midnight encounter near the warehouse, but the dockside ledger evidence and Mara’s testimony pivot the narrative toward a planned, controlled strike by Silas. The evidence sequence—from time-window alignment and weapon type to documentary proof of illicit shipments and Silas’s own signature—collectively constrains the suspect pool to Silas, while sidelining alternatives with weaker or conflicting alibis. Finn’s and Mara’s other observations help triangulate the timeline and corroborate the presence of a limping man with knowledge of the warehouse perimeter, further tightening Silas’s culpability.

In exoneration terms, Deputy Finn’s official duties at the docks, and his potentially corroborated presence elsewhere during the same window, render him unlikely to be the killer. Mara’s testimony, while helping identify Silas, does not implicate her directly; she lacks a direct access path to the murder weapon and the private cargo network integrated with Silas’s ledger. Rex Bowman’s role, if any, remains unclear from the current evidence; nothing directly ties him to the midnight delivery or the confrontation at the warehouse. The evidence collectively narrows guilt away from these figures, reinforcing Silas Vane as the principal actor behind the crime and the most likely culprit given the integrated documentary and eyewitness lines.

input.content:
You receive a handoff document written by a detective at the end of their investigation.
The document may be organised in any way the detective chose. Your job is to extract the
CommitDecision schema from it precisely.

culprit_id   — the char_* ID the detective named as the killer. Must match exactly.
means_id     — the means_* ID the detective named. Must match exactly.
evidence_notes — one {"clue_id": str, "note": str} object per key clue in the handoff.
                 Trim each note to its core factual claim (~120 words max); cut padding
                 and repetition but preserve the substance. Do not add, invent, or merge notes.
process_explanation — copy the detective's narrative prose. If it exceeds 140 words, trim it
                     to under 140 words by cutting the least essential sentences — preserve
                     the verdict, core evidence chain, and exoneration; cut elaboration first.

If the handoff is ambiguous about a field, extract the most explicit value present.
Do not infer or hallucinate values that are not in the document.

EVIDENCE NOTES:
clue_01 — The victim is Aldric Marsh, harbor merchant, age 47. This identification anchors the case: Marsh’s role as a dockside entrepreneur means he moved valuable goods through the Riverside and Harbor districts, placing him in proximity to both legitimate trade and illicit cargo networks. Establishing his occupation and age helps assess motive and opportunity, since a figure involved in risky shipments would have both allies and enemies among those who benefit from the trade or from underhanded deals. It also frames the time window of the crime: someone who knew Marsh’s routines could exploit his confidence around dawn when ships were switching cargo. The detail grounds the investigation in a concrete social and economic position that links suspect networks to the crime scene.

clue_02 — The cause of death is a single deep blade wound to the chest, consistent with a fixed-blade knife. This physical clue narrows the mechanism of the murder and points to a weapon capable of delivering a decisive, fatal stab. In practical terms, it excludes more leisurely methods (poison, rope as strangulation) and ties the crime to a hands-on assault. The rigidity of a fixed blade also implies premeditation or a direct confrontation rather than a quick, accidental contact. With a blade as the murder instrument, the killer’s access to a personal knife or a weapon readily available at the docks becomes a plausible line of inquiry, linking weapon choice to a likely local actor.

clue_03 — Time of death is estimated between midnight and 2 a.m., based on body temperature. This narrows the critical window for the crime and constrains suspects to those active or in proximity during those hours. It harmonizes with other time-based clues, such as witness reports or ledger events occurring around that period. In practical terms, any alibi or plan tied to the hours around midnight must be tested against Marsh’s routine and the suspects’ movements: who could be at the docks at that late hour, who had access to the Riverside Warehouse, and who stood to gain from Marsh’s death if a midnight shipment mask was involved?

clue_F1 — Deputy Finn found the body at first light near Pier 3; Marsh had been there for a few hours and the blood had darkened. This corroborates the death window inferred from Marsh’s body temperature and places the scene at Pier 3 as the primary site of death. Finn’s observation that the body had been there for hours supports a late-night arrival of Marsh or a late-night confrontation there. The location near Pier 3 also aligns with the docks’ transport routes, suggesting someone familiar with maritime traffic could have approached Marsh during a quiet moment at dawn. This testimony helps calibrate the narrative timeline and narrows the geography of the crime.

clue_F2 — Finn notes Marsh had a reputation for clean dealings; dock hands criticized him for underpaying. This establishes Marsh’s social and economic context: he was a mercantile figure whose business practices could create friction with workers, rivals, or associates who resented him. While not a direct link to the killer, it broadens the field of possible suspects by highlighting motive-based tensions tied to the docks’ labor and shipping economy. It also seeds potential grievances that could be exploited by someone aiming to silence Marsh to protect a clandestine operation or illegal cargo. The social backdrop matters to motive construction.

clue_04 — The Shipping Ledger shows a midnight delivery of bonded cargo to the riverside warehouse, authorised and signed for by the harbormaster’s deputy, Silas Vane. This is a pivotal connection: it directly ties Silas to a midnight shipment at the riverside warehouse, placing him at the center of both Marsh’s death window and the illegal cargo dynamic. The fact that the delivery was bonded and officially signed for by Silas suggests either his control over or complicity within illicit operations, creating a powerful motive to silence Marsh if the latter threatened exposure. This clue shifts the investigation toward Silas as a principal actor with access to the critical locations involved in the crime.

clue_05 — The handwriting on the ledger matches Silas Vane’s known signature on the front page. This strengthens the inference that Silas authored or authorized the midnight delivery entry, elevating him from a potential collaborator to a person with direct involvement in the illicit cargo operation. A signature match is a high-confidence link that translates into a concrete, documentary proof of Silas’s control over the transaction recorded in the ledger. That control would afford him both motive and opportunity to confront Marsh if Marsh threatened to expose the operation. The handwriting match consolidates the case against Silas.

clue_M1 — Mara saw a tall man with a pronounced limp crossing toward the warehouse gates around midnight; he moved quickly, as if trying not to be seen. This is a crucial eyewitness fragment that aligns with the surveillance of Silas’s characteristics and the timing of midnight activity near the Riverside Warehouse. The description of a limp provides a distinctive physical feature that Mara could plausibly identify later, tying the observed individual to someone known to us—most strongly Silas, given his later associations with the ledger. The sighting places a person matching Silas’s profile at the threshold of the crime scene during Marsh’s late-night peril, reinforcing the likelihood of a confrontation around the warehouse perimeter.

clue_M2 — The ledger Mara shows is Silas’s own ledger for private cargo runs; Mara also identifies the limp as Silas and declares it was him. This is especially damning: it directly links Silas to the private cargo network through his own records and confirms what Mara observed about the limp. It transforms a vague eyewitness memory into a documentary-alibi-weakening fact: Silas’s private ledger proves his involvement in illicit shipments, and Mara’s identification of him as the limping man at the warehouse gates ties him physically to the midnight crossing. This combination of documentary and eyewitness evidence forms a turning point in the investigation.

clue_W1 — A search warrant signed by the magistrate authorized entry to bonded warehouses on the dock district. This credential shows there was official government permission to inspect key locations, and it legitimizes the potential access Silas would have had to the Riverside Warehouse and its ledgers. It also frames the ability of a law-enforcement-adjacent actor to move within the warehouses to secure or conceal contraband shipments. The warrant’s existence corroborates the plausibility of the midnight delivery trace and supports the possibility that those with access to the warehouses—like Silas—could orchestrate or obstruct exposure. It underlines procedural context rather than direct guilt, but it strengthens the chain of custody around the relevant sites.

clue_S1 — Silas Vane’s claim: he was at the office finishing month-end accounts all evening, left around ten, and went straight home; he denies knowledge of any midnight delivery. This is a guarded claim that must be weighed against other converging clues. The contradiction with the ledger showing a midnight shipment authorized by Silas casts doubt on his alibi and raises questions about his presence or control over illicit operations at or near the Riverside Warehouse during the covered window. The alibi creates a narrative tension: either he is lying or the ledger is forged or misused. It’s a critical node in the investigation, demanding further corroboration or contradiction through other witnesses or documents.

PROCESS EXPLANATION:
The killer is Silas Vane (char_silas), Harbormaster’s deputy, who used a blade to kill Aldric Marsh at the Riverside Warehouse area near the docks, in the midnight-to-dawn window. The evidence points to a carefully staged confrontation tied to a clandestine cargo scheme: a ledger of midnight bonded deliveries shows Silas authorised and recorded shipments to the riverside warehouse, and the handwriting on the ledger matches Silas’s own signature, indicating his control over the operation. Mara’s testimony about a limping man crossing toward the warehouse at midnight, reinforced by her identification of Silas as the limping figure, links him physically to the scene. Marsh’s body bears a fatal blade wound, consistent with a personal knife, aligning with the confrontation rather than a distant plot. The discovery of Silas’s private ledger and his contested alibi further erode any defense that he was merely a bystander.

The motive centers on silencing a potential exposé of the illicit cargo run; Marsh’s death could be the result of his threat to reveal the operation. The scene was prepared to resemble a random midnight encounter near the warehouse, but the dockside ledger evidence and Mara’s testimony pivot the narrative toward a planned, controlled strike by Silas. The evidence sequence—from time-window alignment and weapon type to documentary proof of illicit shipments and Silas’s own signature—collectively constrains the suspect pool to Silas, while sidelining alternatives with weaker or conflicting alibis. Finn’s and Mara’s other observations help triangulate the timeline and corroborate the presence of a limping man with knowledge of the warehouse perimeter, further tightening Silas’s culpability.

In exoneration terms, Deputy Finn’s official duties at the docks, and his potentially corroborated presence elsewhere during the same window, render him unlikely to be the killer. Mara’s testimony, while helping identify Silas, does not implicate her directly; she lacks a direct access path to the murder weapon and the private cargo network integrated with Silas’s ledger. Rex Bowman’s role, if any, remains unclear from the current evidence; nothing directly ties him to the midnight delivery or the confrontation at the warehouse. The evidence collectively narrows guilt away from these figures, reinforcing Silas Vane as the principal actor behind the crime and the most likely culprit given the integrated documentary and eyewitness lines.

evidence_notes.note: The victim is identified as Aldric Marsh, a 47-year-old harbor merchant. His dockside trade role places him around Riverside/Harbor cargo activity and makes conflicts over shipments and deals plausible, especially for someone familiar with his routines.
Cause of death is a single deep blade wound to the chest, consistent with a fixed-blade knife, indicating a close, hands-on stabbing rather than poison or strangulation.
Time of death is estimated between midnight and 2 a.m. based on body temperature, narrowing the crime window to late-night dock/warehouse activity.
Deputy Finn found the body at first light near Pier 3; the blood had darkened and the body had been there for hours. This supports a late-night death and places the primary scene near Pier 3.
Finn reports Marsh was viewed as generally clean in dealings but criticized by dock hands for underpaying, establishing potential motive tensions tied to dock labor and commerce.

A shipping ledger records a midnight delivery of bonded cargo to the riverside warehouse, authorized and signed for by Harbormaster’s deputy Silas Vane, tying Silas to activity within the death window and location context.
Handwriting on the ledger matches Silas Vane’s known signature on the front page, strengthening that Silas personally authored/authorized the midnight delivery entry.

Mara saw a tall man with a pronounced limp crossing toward the warehouse gates around midnight, moving quickly as if avoiding notice—placing a distinctive figure at the warehouse at the relevant time.

Mara states the ledger she shows is Silas’s private cargo-run ledger and identifies the limping man as Silas, directly linking him to illicit shipments and physically to the warehouse vicinity at midnight.
A magistrate-signed search warrant authorized entry to bonded warehouses in the dock district, establishing official authority/procedural access to inspect those locations.
Silas claims he stayed at the office all evening, left around ten, went straight home, and denies any midnight delivery—an alibi contradicted by the ledger evidence.

content.process_explanation: The killer is Silas Vane (char_silas), Harbormaster’s deputy, who used a blade to kill Aldric Marsh at the Riverside Warehouse area near the docks, in the midnight-to-dawn window. A ledger shows a midnight bonded delivery authorized and recorded by Silas, and the handwriting matches his signature. Mara saw a limping man at the warehouse around midnight and identifies him as Silas, and her ledger ties him to private cargo runs. Marsh’s fatal chest wound matches a fixed-blade knife, fitting a close confrontation. Silas’s alibi is undermined by these records and sightings; Finn and Mara are not directly implicated, and nothing ties Rex Bowman to the midnight delivery.

message_history.content:
EVIDENCE NOTES:
clue_01 — The victim is Aldric Marsh, harbor merchant, age 47. This identification anchors the case: Marsh’s role as a dockside entrepreneur means he moved valuable goods through the Riverside and Harbor districts, placing him in proximity to both legitimate trade and illicit cargo networks. Establishing his occupation and age helps assess motive and opportunity, since a figure involved in risky shipments would have both allies and enemies among those who benefit from the trade or from underhanded deals. It also frames the time window of the crime: someone who knew Marsh’s routines could exploit his confidence around dawn when ships were switching cargo. The detail grounds the investigation in a concrete social and economic position that links suspect networks to the crime scene.

output.process_explanation: The killer is Silas Vane (char_silas), Harbormaster’s deputy, who used a blade to kill Aldric Marsh at the Riverside Warehouse area near the docks, in the midnight-to-dawn window. A ledger shows a midnight bonded delivery authorized and recorded by Silas, and the handwriting matches his signature. Mara saw a limping man at the warehouse around midnight and identifies him as Silas, and her ledger ties him to private cargo runs. Marsh’s fatal chest wound matches a fixed-blade knife, fitting a close confrontation. Silas’s alibi is undermined by these records and sightings; Finn and Mara are not directly implicated, and nothing ties Rex Bowman to the midnight delivery.
